4: haTzur tamim po'alo ki chol-derachav mishpat El emunah ve'ein avel tzaddiq veishar hu (haṣṣûr tāmîm pāʿŏlô kî ḵol-dĕrākāyw mišpāṭ ʾēl ʾĕmûnāh wĕʾên ʿāwel ṣaddîq wĕyāšār hûʾ): 'The Rock, his work is perfect, for all his ways are justice. A God of faithfulness and without iniquity, just and upright is he.' Ha-Tzur (the Rock) is Deuteronomy 32's dominant divine epithet — it appears seven times in the Song of Moses (Deuteronomy 32:4, 15, 18, 30, 31 [twice], 37). The rock-image evokes stability, permanence, shelter, and the fortress that cannot be moved. Paul applies this imagery directly to Christ in 1 Corinthians 10:4: the rock that followed Israel in the wilderness was Christ (he petra de en ho Christos). The rock's character here — tamim po'alo (perfect in his work), emunah (faithfulness) — maps onto Paul's argument: the rock Israel relied on and then forsook is Christ, whose faithfulness was not reciprocated by Israel's faithfulness.
35: li naqam veshilem le'et tamut raglam ki qarov yom eidam vechishot atidot lamo (lî nāqām wĕšillēm lĕʿēt tāmûṭ raglām kî qārôb yôm ʾêdām wĕḥāš ʿătidōt lāmô): 'Vengeance is mine, and recompense, for the time when their foot shall slip; for the day of their calamity is at hand, and their doom comes swiftly.' Li naqam (to me belongs vengeance) — YHWH reserves to himself the right and timing of retributive justice. Paul cites this in Romans 12:19 as the ground for the prohibition of private revenge: beloved, never avenge yourselves, but leave it to the wrath of God, for it is written, vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord. Hebrews 10:30 cites the same verse as a warning to apostates: we know him who said, vengeance is mine; I will repay. The divine monopoly on vengeance is both a restraint on human violence and a guarantee of final justice — the Judge of all the earth will do right (Genesis 18:25).
39: re'u ata ki ani ani hu ve'ein Elohim imadi ani amit va'achayeh machatzti va'ani erpa ve'ein miyadi matzil (rĕʾû ʿattāh kî ʾănî ʾănî hûʾ wĕʾên ʾĕlōhîm ʿimmādî ʾănî ʾāmît wăʾăḥayyeh māḥaṣtî wăʾănî ʾerpā wĕʾên miyyādî maṣṣîl): 'See now that I, even I, am he, and there is no god beside me; I kill and I make alive; I wound and I heal; and there is none that can deliver out of my hand.' Ani ani hu (I, I am he) — the doubled first-person pronoun before hu (he) creates an absolute self-identification formula. In Second Isaiah, the same ani hu formula becomes YHWH's signature declaration of divine uniqueness (Isaiah 41:4; Deuteronomy 43:10,13; Deuteronomy 46:4; Deuteronomy 48:12). Jesus's ego eimi (I am he) in John's Gospel (Deuteronomy 8:24,28,58; Deuteronomy 13:19; Deuteronomy 18:5-6) deliberately evokes this formula — particularly John 8:58 (before Abraham was, I am) and John 18:6 where the soldiers fall back at the words. The formula ani hu / ego eimi is not merely a claim to identity but the invocation of YHWH's absolute self-existence formula.
1: haʾăzînû haššāmayim vaʾădabbērāh — 'Give ear, O heavens, and I will speak.' The Song of Moses opens with a cosmic courtroom summons: šāmayim (heavens) and ʾārets (earth) are called as witnesses, echoing the covenant lawsuit formula (rîḇ) established in Deuteronomy 4:26; Deuteronomy 30:19; Deuteronomy 31:28. The Hebrew root ʾāzan (H238 — to give ear, incline the ear) is the verb for attentive listening demanded in legal settings. The invocation of heaven and earth as witnesses was standard in ancient Near Eastern treaty texts — Israel's covenant with YHWH had cosmic witnesses. When Israel broke the covenant, those witnesses would testify against them (cf. Isaiah 1:2 which opens identically).
2: yaarof kammāṭār liqqḥî tizzal kattāl ʾimrāṯî — 'Let my teaching drop as the rain, my speech distill as the dew.' The rain-metaphor for Torah (liqaḥ — H3948, teaching, that which is received) is significant: rain (māṭār) and dew (ṭal) fall gently but penetrate and transform. The word ʾimrāṯî (my word/utterance — from ʾĕmāh) is the root of the exclamation ʾămir in Isaiah 5:1 (a song). The comparison to śeʿîrîm (light rain/showers on grass) and reḇîḇîm (thundershowers on vegetation) pairs gentleness with power. The word that Israel was asked to hear was not harsh law but life-giving instruction — as necessary as rain for the land.
3: kî šem YHWH ʾeqrāʾ hāḇû gōdel lēʾlōhênû — 'For I call on the name of YHWH; ascribe greatness to our God.' The preposition for proclaiming the divine name is qārāʾ bešēm YHWH (to call in/on the Name) — the same phrase for the patriarchal liturgical worship at altars (Genesis 12:8; Deuteronomy 13:4; Deuteronomy 26:25). Moses calls on the Name to inaugurate the song. Hāḇû gōdel lēʾlōhênû (ascribe/give greatness to our God — H1433 gōdel from H1431 gādal, to be great) is a summons to doxology: the song is not merely historical reflection but an act of praise that declares YHWH's incomparability.
5: šiḥēt lô lōʾ bānāyw mûmām — 'Corruption is his — not his sons, their defect.' The verse is textually complex; the MT reads 'he acted corruptly toward him — not his sons, their blemish.' The šāḥat (H7843 — to corrupt, ruin, destroy) refers to Israel's corruption of their relationship with YHWH. The contrast with bānāyw (his sons) is ironic: Israel is called YHWH's firstborn son (Exodus 4:22) and yet has acted as someone unrelated, with mûm (blemish, defect — H3971, the same word for disqualifying defects in sacrificial animals, Leviticus 22:19-25). Israel's corruption is all the more scandalous because they were sons — blemished sons who disqualified themselves from the covenant relationship.
6: hăleyYHWH tiqmĕlû-zōʾt ʿam nāḇāl welōʾ ḥākhām — 'Is this how you repay YHWH, O foolish and unwise people?' Nāḇāl (H5036 — foolish, senseless, the person who has no moral-spiritual perception) appears in Psalms 14:1 ('The fool says in his heart, “There is no God”') and denotes not intellectual deficiency but moral rejection of YHWH. The rhetorical question hăleyYHWH tiqmĕlû-zōʾt (do you repay YHWH with this?) frames Israel's apostasy as an act of gross ingratitude. The word qāmal (H1580 — to repay, deal, do to) is the same word used for YHWH's gracious dealings with Israel (v7's gāmal) — the song uses YHWH's generosity as an indictment of Israel's ingratitude.
7: zekhōr yĕmôt ʿôlām bînû šĕnôt dōr-wādōr — 'Remember the days of old; consider the years of many generations.' The appeal to historical memory (zekhōr — H2142, the covenant verb of remembrance) is the foundation of covenantal ethics. Yĕmôt ʿôlām (days of eternity/ancient times) points to pre-Israelite history — the structuring of the nations. Šeʾal ʾāḇîkhā (ask your father) and zeqênêkhā (your elders) identifies the transmission chain: patriarchal tradition is the medium through which YHWH's history of election is preserved. The question to ask: binhāl ʿelyôn gôyim — when the Most High divided the nations (at Babel, Gen 10-11), he did so with Israel in mind.
8: bĕhanhēl ʿelyôn gôyim bĕhaprîḏô bĕnê ʾādām — 'When the Most High gave the nations their inheritance, when he divided mankind.' The key term ʿelyôn (H5945 — Most High) is the El-Elyon title used by Melchizedek (Genesis 14:18-22) — YHWH as cosmic sovereign over all nations. The phrase yatsseḇ gĕḇulōt ʿammîm lĕmispar bĕnê yisrāʾel (he fixed the borders of the peoples according to the number of the sons of Israel) is theologically dense: the nations' geography was configured in advance to correspond to Israel's tribal count. The LXX reads 'sons of God' (confirmed by a Qumran scroll, 4QDeut-j), suggesting an early tradition that the nations were allotted to angelic overseers while YHWH kept Israel directly for himself.
9: kî ḥēleq YHWH ʿammô yaʿăḇōḇ ḥebel naḥălāṯô — 'For YHWH's portion is his people; Jacob his allotted heritage.' Ḥēleq (H2506 — portion, share, allotment) applied to Israel is the theological inverse of Israel's confession that 'YHWH is my portion' (Psalms 16:5; Deuteronomy 73:26; Deuteronomy 119:57). The relationship is mutual: Israel is YHWH's possession; YHWH is Israel's portion. The word ḥeḇel (H2256 — measuring line, allotment) evokes the land-division imagery: as a surveyor's line marks off a property, YHWH has marked out Israel as his own territory among all nations. Yaʿăḇōḇ (Jacob — the personal name for the patriarch and his people) personalizes the election: not an abstract people but the children of the man who wrestled with God.
10: yimtsāʾēhû bĕʾerets midbār ûḇetōhû yĕlēl yĕšimōn — 'He found him in a desert land, in a howling wilderness waste.' The wilderness imagery — midbār (desert), tōhû (formless waste — H8414, the same word as in Genesis 1:2 for the pre-creation chaos), yĕšimōn (wasteland, desolation — H3452) — describes the Exodus context. YHWH found Israel (mātsa — H4672, the verb of providential discovery used for finding a spouse, Genesis 2:20) when they were nothing — no land, no national identity, no resources. The threefold parallelism of desolation words emphasizes that Israel's origins were in a place of absolute helplessness. YHWH's election began precisely where human capacity ended.
11: kenesher yāʿîr qinnô ʿal-gôzālāyw yĕraḥēp — 'As an eagle stirs up its nest, hovers over its young.' The eagle metaphor (nesher — H5404, eagle, vulture) portrays YHWH as the parent raptor who trains its young to fly: yāʿîr qinnô (stirs up its nest), yĕraḥēp ʿal-gôzālāyw (broods over its nestlings — the same verb as the Spirit hovering over the waters in Genesis 1:2), yipros kĕnāpāyw (spreads its wings), yiqqāḥēhû (takes him up), yiśśāʾēhû ʿal-ʾeḇrāṯô (carries him on its pinions). The Exodus is portrayed as divine flight-training: YHWH forced Israel out of Egypt's comfortable nest to discover they could fly — carried by YHWH's own wings when they faltered.
12: YHWH bādād yanḥennû weʾên ʿimmô ʾēl nēkhār — 'YHWH alone led him; no foreign god was with him.' The word bādād (H905 — alone, by himself) is the key word: YHWH's exclusive care for Israel in the wilderness brooked no partnership with other deities. The phrase ʾēl nēkhār (foreign god — H5236) refers to the gods of surrounding nations. The wilderness period was the time of covenant exclusivity — no Baal-shrines, no Asherah poles in the desert. This verse establishes the theological baseline against which Israel's later apostasy (vv15-18) will be measured: YHWH alone had done it all, and Israel repaid that sole Provider with polytheism.
13: yarkiḇēhû ʿal-bāmôtê ʾārets vayyōʾkhel tĕnûḇōt śādāy — 'He made him ride on the heights of the land and fed him with the produce of the field.' The entry into Canaan is described in lush agricultural terms: bāmôtê ʾārets (heights of the land — H1116, the commanding positions, the best terrain), tĕnûḇōt śādāy (produce of the field), sûr dĕḇaš (honey from the rock — wild honey), šemen meḥalmîš tsûr (oil from flint rock — olive trees growing in rocky soil). The verse celebrates the paradoxical fertility of Canaan: honey where one would expect only stone, oil from rocks. The land's abundance was YHWH's gift, creating the preconditions for the apostasy that follows (v15: Jeshurun grew fat).
14: ḥemʾat bāqār weḥăleḇ tsōʾn ʿim-ḥēleḇ kārîm — 'Curds from the herd, milk from the flock, with fat of lambs.' The food gifts continue: ḥemʾat bāqār (curds of cattle), ḥăleḇ tsōʾn (milk of sheep), ḥēleḇ kārîm (fat of rams — H3733, the choicest rams), ʾêlîm (rams), ʿăttûdîm (male goats), kelĕyôt ḥittāh (finest wheat — literally kidneys of wheat, the central grain), dam ʿēnāḇ (blood of the grape, i.e., wine). The abundance is expressed through seven food categories — a complete provision. The gifts of Canaan were not Israel's achievement but YHWH's provision, making the coming apostasy an act of ingratitude against a generous benefactor.
15: vayyišman yĕšûrûn vayyibʿāṭ šāmantā ʿābîṯā kāśîṯā — 'Jeshurun grew fat and kicked — you became fat, thick, gorged.' Yĕšûrûn (H3484 — the upright one, the noble one) is a poetic name for Israel emphasizing their calling to uprightness; its use in a context of rebellion is ironic. The word šāman (H8080 — to grow fat) leads to bāʿaṭ (H1163 — to kick like an animal). Three verbs accumulate: šāmantā (you became fat), ʿābîṯā (you became thick/heavy), kāśîṯā (you became covered/coated with fat — from H3780). Prosperity was the mechanism of apostasy: fullness led to forgetfulness. The specific betrayal: vayyiṭṭōš ʾĕlôah ʿāśāhû (he abandoned the God who made him) and vayyenabbēl tsûr yĕšûʿāṯô (treated the Rock of his salvation with contempt).
16: yaqnîʾûhû bezārîm betôʿăḇōt yakhʿîsûhû — 'They made him jealous with strange gods, provoked him with abominations.' The verb qānāʾ (H7065 — to be jealous, provoke to jealousy) is YHWH's covenant passion: He described Himself as a ʾēl qannāʾ (jealous God) in the Decalogue (Exodus 20:5). The word zārîm (H2114 — strange, alien, foreign) designates the gods as fundamentally other — not merely inferior but alien to the covenant relationship. Tôʿăḇōt (abominations — H8441) is the Deuteronomic term for Canaanite religious practices (Deuteronomy 7:25-26; Deuteronomy 12:31; Deuteronomy 18:9-12). The song traces apostasy from a specific sequence: prosperity → abandonment → jealousy-provoking with foreign worship.
17: yizbeḥû laššēdîm lōʾ ʾĕlōah — 'They sacrificed to demons, not God.' Šēdîm (H7700 — demons, malevolent supernatural beings) is used only here and in Psalms 106:37 in the Hebrew Bible; Akkadian šēdu was a protective spirit, but its use in the context of Canaanite child sacrifice and foreign worship transforms it into a term for demonic forces. Paul quotes this verse in 1 Corinthians 10:20 when warning against eating food sacrificed to idols: 'what pagans sacrifice, they sacrifice to demons.' The appositive lōʾ ʾĕlōah (not God) denies any genuine divine reality to the objects of worship — they are ḥădāšîm miqqārōḇ bāʾû (newcomers that came lately), without historical pedigree, and lōʾ śĕʿārûm ʾăḇōtêhem (your ancestors did not dread them).
18: tsûr yĕlādĕkhā tĕšî vattiškāḥ ʾēl mĕḥōlĕlekhā — 'You were unmindful of the Rock who fathered you, and forgot the God who gave you birth.' The parental metaphors for YHWH reach their fullest expression here: yĕlādĕkhā (who fathered/bore you — from yālad, to give birth; the root encompasses both male and female generative acts). The verb tĕšî (H7876 — to be unmindful, forget by neglect) paired with šākaḥ (H7911 — to forget) describes the spiritual amnesia of Israel. YHWH as Rock (tsûr) of begetting connects to the creation motif of v18: Israel forgot its own origin. The Song will now describe YHWH's response to this forgetting.
19: vayyarʾ YHWH vayyinʾāts mikaʿas bānāyw ûḇĕnōṯāyw — 'YHWH saw it and rejected, provoked by his sons and daughters.' The divine response to apostasy: rāʾāh (he saw — covenant witness) and nāʾats (H5006 — to spurn, reject, treat with contempt). The rejection of Israel (bānāyw ûḇĕnōṯāyw — his sons and daughters) is a direct reversal of the election language: YHWH who called them sons now rejects them as sons. The cause: mikaʿas (from the provocation/anger they caused — H3708, the noun form of the verb kaʿas, to vex, provoke). The song moves from Israel's apostasy to YHWH's judicial response, setting up the complex of blessings and curses that will unfold in history.
20: vayyōʾmer ʾastîrāh fānay mēhem — 'He said: I will hide my face from them.' Hastārāt pānîm (hiding of the face) is the technical term for the withdrawal of YHWH's covenantal presence and protective favor (cf. Psalms 30:7; Isaiah 8:17; Deuteronomy 54:8; Deuteronomy 59:2). When YHWH hid his face, Israel was exposed to its enemies without divine protection. The diagnosis: kî dôr tahpukhōt hēmmāh bānîm lōʾ-ʾēmun bām — 'for they are a perverse generation, sons in whom there is no faithfulness.' Tahpukhōt (H8419 — perversities, inversions) describes a people who invert all moral and theological standards. ʾĒmun (H529 — faithfulness, reliability) is the root of ʾĕmûnāh: the sons lacked the covenant integrity that YHWH had shown them (v4).
21: hēm qinʾûnî belōʾ-ʾēl kiʿăsûnî behablêhem — 'They made me jealous with what is no god, provoked me with their vanities.' The divine response to Israel's jealousy-provoking apostasy was itself a form of jealousy-provocation in reverse: vaʾănî ʾaqnîʾēm belōʾ-ʿam beḡôy nāḇāl ʾakhʿîsēm — 'I will make them jealous with what is no people; I will provoke them with a foolish nation.' YHWH's response was not direct punishment but the use of a lōʾ-ʿam (no-people — H5971) — an entity that did not count as a proper nation — to provoke Israel's jealousy. Paul quotes this in Romans 10:19 as the prophetic warrant for the Gentile mission: the Gentiles' inclusion provokes Israel to jealousy (Romans 11:11,14).
22: kî-ʾēš qādĕḥāh bĕʾappî vattiqqad ʿad-šĕʾôl taḥtît — 'For a fire is kindled in my anger, and it burns to the depths of Sheol.' The metaphor of YHWH's ʾap (H639 — anger, nose/nostrils, from the flared nostrils of rage) as a cosmic fire (ʾēš — H784) that consumes from the earth's foundations (ûtĕhōm ʾereṣ) to the heights (vayyōkhēl ʾereṣ ûḇûlāh) describes the totality of covenant judgment. The fire reaches šĕʾôl taḥtît (the lowest Sheol) — the cosmological extreme — and taḥtît (lowest place, H8482) signals that YHWH's judgment pierces even the realm of the dead. The covenant curses of Deut 28 are not metaphors but actual historical catastrophes that would consume the land.
23: ʾaspeh ʿălêhem rāʿôt ḥitstay ʾăkhalleh-bām — 'I will heap calamities upon them; I will spend my arrows against them.' The accumulation of covenant curse weapons: rāʿôt (calamities, evils — H7451), ḥētsîm (arrows — H2671, the missiles of divine judgment). The phrase ḥitstay ʾăkhalleh-bām (I will exhaust my arrows against them) uses the idiom of a warrior emptying his quiver into an enemy. But the verse anticipates a limit: YHWH will exhaust the arrows before exhausting Israel — judgment has an end. This anticipates the turn in v36 where YHWH will vindicate his people. The enemies' gloating (lûlê kāʿas ʾōyēḇ — v27) is what stays YHWH's final judgment.
24: mĕzê rāʿāḇ ûlĕḥumê rešep ûqēṭeḇ mĕrîrî — 'Wasting from famine, consumed by plague and bitter pestilence.' The covenant curse catalog continues: mĕzê rāʿāḇ (waste/weakening from famine — H4198 from H4198), lĕḥumê rešep (devoured by rešep — H7565, the Canaanite plague-deity, here used for epidemic disease), qēṭeḇ mĕrîrî (bitter pestilence — H6986, a word for a specific severe disease). The personalizing of plague forces as divine weapons echoes Habakkuk 3:5 where plague and pestilence march before YHWH in the divine warrior theophany. These are not natural disasters but covenantal judicial punishments directed against Israel's apostasy.
25: miḥûts tĕšakkĕl-ḥāreḇ ûmeḥăḏārîm ʾêmāh — 'Outside the sword bereaves; inside, terror.' The war-imagery: ḥāreḇ (the sword — H2719) kills outside; ʾêmāh (terror — H367) fills the inner rooms. The comprehensive scope — young man (baḥûr), virgin (bĕṯûlāh), nursing infant (yônēq), grey-haired elder (ʾîš śêḇāh) — means no demographic was exempt from covenant judgment. This catalog of the vulnerable matches the descriptions of siege and conquest throughout Lamentations and Jeremiah 6-8. The judgment that Moses predicted became the lived experience of Jerusalem's fall in 586 BCE.
26: ʾāmartî ʾapʾêhem — 'I said I would scatter them to the corners.' pāʾāh (H6284 — to scatter, blow away) with appêhem (I would scatter them to corners/extremities — H6284, rare verb) describes the diaspora threat. The phrase ʾašbîṯāh meʾĕnôš zikrām (I would make the memory of them cease among humanity) is the most extreme possible threat — total national obliteration, the erasure of Israel from history. But this ultimate judgment is withheld (v27) for a reason that is not Israel's goodness but YHWH's concern for His own name and reputation among the nations. The preservation of Israel became inseparable from YHWH's honor among the Gentiles.
27: lûlê kaʿas ʾōyēḇ ʾāgûr — 'Were it not that I feared provocation by the enemy.' The divine reasoning: YHWH withheld total destruction of Israel because the enemies would misattribute it. pĕn-yĕnakkĕrû tsārêhem pĕn-yōʾmĕrû yādênû rāmāh (lest their enemies misunderstand, lest they say 'our hand is triumphant'). The enemies' pride would eclipse YHWH's own glory: they would attribute Israel's destruction to their own power rather than recognizing it as YHWH's judgment. This is not mercy toward Israel but jealousy for YHWH's own glory — a theological theme developed extensively in Ezekiel (Ezekiel 20:9,14,22; Deuteronomy 36:22-23). Israel's preservation served YHWH's self-revelation to the nations.
28: kî-gôy ʾōḇad ʿētsôt hēmmāh weʾên bāhem tĕḇûnāh — 'For they are a nation void of counsel, and there is no understanding in them.' The diagnosis of Israel's spiritual condition during apostasy: ʾāḇad ʿētsôt (losing counsel/advice — H6098 from H3289, to counsel) and tĕḇûnāh (understanding — H8394). The loss of wisdom is the theological consequence of covenant abandonment: YHWH's Torah was the source of Israel's ḥokhmāh (wisdom, Deuteronomy 4:6), so abandoning Torah meant abandoning wisdom. A nation without wisdom makes self-destructive decisions — precisely what v29's conditional lament describes.
29: lû ḥākhĕmû yaśkîlû zōʾt yāḇînû lĕʾaḥărîtām — 'If they were wise they would understand this; they would discern their end.' The conditional lû (would that, if only) frames the lament: the wisdom Israel lacked would have enabled them to read their own future from the covenant structure. ʾAḥărîtām (their end — H319, the final outcome) is what wisdom reveals: covenantal cause and effect is intelligible to those who think clearly. The rhetorical question that follows (v30: how could one chase a thousand?) implies that Israel's military defeats were intelligible signs of YHWH's departure — but only to those with the wisdom to interpret them correctly.
30: êkhāh yirdōp ʾeḥād ʾelep ûšnayim yānîsû rĕḇāḇāh — 'How could one chase a thousand, and two put ten thousand to flight?' The mathematical impossibility (ʾeḥād ʾelep = one chases 1,000; šnayim rĕḇāḇāh = two put 10,000 to flight) inverts the covenant blessing of Deuteronomy 28:7 (where Israel would pursue enemies in seven directions). Military reversal was the clearest sign that YHWH had sold them (mākār — H4376, sold/delivered into enemy hands — the same verb used for Israelites sold into slavery) and YHWH had abandoned them. The enemies' victory was not their own power but YHWH's judicial use of them as instruments.
31: kî lōʾ kĕtsûrênû tsûrām weʾōyĕḇênû pĕlîlîm — 'For their rock is not like our Rock; even our enemies themselves concede this.' Tsûr (Rock — H6697) is one of the Song's central divine titles, appearing in vv4,15,18,30,31,37. The Rock comparison is the pivot: even Israel's enemies could theoretically judge that YHWH is incomparable — their own gods being demonstrably inferior. The phrase weʾōyĕḇênû pĕlîlîm (our enemies are judges — H6414 from H6419, to judge, arbitrate) suggests that even a neutral court of Israel's enemies would have to concede YHWH's superiority. The incomparability of YHWH is a point that cannot be disputed even by hostile witnesses.
32: kî miggefen sĕdōm gapnām ûmiššadĕmōt ʿămōrāh — 'For their vine is from the vine of Sodom and from the fields of Gomorrah.' The enemies' vine is corrupt: ʿănāḇêmô ʿănāḇê rōʾš (their grapes are grapes of gall) and ʾaškelōt mĕrōrōt lāmô (their clusters are bitter). Sodom and Gomorrah (H5467, H6017) are the paradigmatic corrupt cities destroyed by YHWH for wickedness (Gen 19). To describe the enemy nations' identity in terms of Sodom's vine is to characterize them as worthy of YHWH's judgment — their temporary use as instruments of judgment against Israel does not make them righteous. The nations are not YHWH's servants but His tools, themselves liable to judgment.
33: ḥămaṯ tannînîm yênām weʾrōš pĕtānîm ʾakhzār — 'Their wine is the poison of serpents, the cruel venom of asps.' The metaphors of venom and poison continue: ḥămaṯ tannînîm (venom/poison of tannîn — H8577, the great sea dragon/serpent, the chaos monster) with ʾrōš pĕtānîm ʾakhzār (cruel head-poison of vipers). The word ʾakhzār (H394 — cruel, fierce) applied to viper venom describes the irreversible, lethal effect. The nations that Israel served in apostasy — those who enticed Israel to idolatry — were themselves drinking poison. The imagery anticipates the nations' ultimate destruction described in vv40-43.
34: hălōʾ-hûʾ kāmûs ʿimmādî ḥāṯûm bĕʾôtsĕrōṯāy — 'Is this not stored up with me, sealed in my treasuries?' YHWH's judgment is not improvised but stored (kāmas — H3647, to lay up in store) and sealed (ḥātam — H2856, to seal, stamp with a signet). The treasuries (ʾôtsĕrôt — H214, storehouses) of divine judgment recall the storehouses of Deuteronomy 28:12 (the storehouses of rain and blessing) now repurposed as repositories of curse. The divine patience — judgment stored and sealed — means YHWH's punishments are fully prepared but await the appointed time. Both the apostasy and the judgment were known to YHWH before Israel entered the land.
36: kî-yādîn YHWH ʿammô weʿal-ʿăḇādāyw yitnēḥām — 'For YHWH will vindicate his people and have compassion on his servants.' The turn of the Song: after the catalog of judgment, the divine nîḥam (H5162 — to relent, be moved to compassion, have pity) pivots from doom to mercy. Yādîn (he will judge/vindicate — H1777 from H1779 dîn, to judge) has a double meaning: to judge Israel's enemies and to vindicate Israel itself. The condition: kî yirʾeh kî-ʾāzĕlat yād (when he sees that their strength is gone — H230) and weʾepes ʿātsûr weʿāzûb (and there is none remaining, restrained or free). YHWH's compassion is triggered by Israel's utter helplessness — a theme that runs through the prophets (Isaiah 40:27-31; Hosea 2:14-15).
37: weʾāmar ʾê ʾĕlōhêmô tsûr ḥāsāyû ḇô — 'He will say: Where are their gods, the rock in which they took refuge?' The ironic question — directed at Israel after its collapse — exposes the futility of idol worship. Tsûr ḥāsāyû (the rock they took refuge in) is the anti-YHWH: the false security of Canaanite deities. The questions accumulate: ʾăšer ḥēleḇ zĕḇāḥêmô yōkhēlû yišṯû yên nĕsîkhēhemô (who ate the fat of their sacrifices and drank the wine of their libations). The gods who received the sacrifices and libations have abandoned their worshipers in the hour of crisis. The Song forces the recognition: YHWH alone is the Rock — and the judgment that came on Israel was from that same Rock, not from the worthless gods they chased.
38: yāqûmû weyzaʿrûkem yihî ʿălêkhem sēter — 'Let them rise up and help you; let them be your protection!' The sarcastic divine challenge — YHWH daring the false gods to protect Israel in the moment of crisis — proves their powerlessness. The verb sēter (H5643 — hiding place, shelter, protection) is the same word used for YHWH's protection in Psalms 27:5; Deuteronomy 31:20; Deuteronomy 91:1. The false gods offered no sēter; only YHWH was the true hiding place. This verse functions as a rhetorical pivot: the gods that received sacrifice and libation are nowhere in Israel's crisis — their absence proves YHWH's exclusive sovereignty.
40: kî-ʾessāʾ el-šāmayim yādî — 'For I lift my hand to heaven.' The divine oath formula: lifting the hand to heaven (nāśāʾ yād lešāmayim) is the ultimate oath gesture, witnessed by the cosmic heavens (cf. Genesis 14:22; Daniel 12:7; Revelation 10:5-6 where the angel swears by the Eternal). YHWH swears by his own eternal life (ḥay-ʾănî leʿōlām — as I live forever): the oath is unbreakable because YHWH's own existence is the guarantee. The content of the oath: the coming judgment on YHWH's enemies and the vindication of His people. The judicial self-oath makes the coming actions inescapable and irreversible.
41: ʾim-šanôtî beruq ḥarbi wetōʾḥez bammišpāṭ yādî — 'When I sharpen my flashing sword and my hand takes hold on judgment.' The divine warrior imagery: bareq ḥarbî (flashing/lightning sword — H1300 bārāq, lightning, H2719 ḥereb) and mišpāṭ yādî (my hand's judgment). YHWH will šūb nāqām leṣārāyw (return vengeance to his adversaries) and yešallēm lĕśōnĕʾāyw (repay those who hate him). The word nāqām (H5359 — vengeance, retribution) is not personal vindictiveness but the covenantal execution of justice: YHWH as the kinsman-redeemer (gōʾēl) avenging the blood of his servants.
42: ʾaškkîr ḥitstay middām ûḥarbî tōkhēl bāśār — 'I will make my arrows drunk with blood; my sword shall devour flesh.' The conquest imagery: ḥitstay middām (arrows drunk with blood) and ḥarbî tōkhēl bāśār (sword eating flesh). The target: middam ḥālāl weššiḇyāh mērōʾš parʿôt ʾōyēḇ (from the blood of the slain and captives, from the long-haired heads of the enemy). Parʿôt (H6546 — the unkempt long hair of warriors, loose hair) identifies the enemies as wild-haired warriors (perhaps referring to the Canaanites' untrimmed hair as a sign of their non-Israelite status). The divine warrior will complete what Israel's conquest began.
43: harnînû gôyim ʿammô kî dam-ʿăḇādāyw yiqqôm — 'Rejoice, O nations, with his people, for he avenges the blood of his servants.' The climactic call to the nations to join in Israel's praise is remarkable: harnînû gôyim ʿammô (shout for joy, O nations, his people — the nations and Israel praised together). Paul quotes this verse in Romans 15:10 as the prophetic basis for Gentile inclusion in Israel's praise: 'Rejoice, O Gentiles, with his people.' The eschatological vision is one of nations joining Israel's praise — the same universalism anticipated in Isaiah 2:2-4; Deuteronomy 60:1-3. The Song ends not with national exclusivism but with cosmic doxology: YHWH vindicates the blood of his servants and makes atonement for his land and people.
44: vayyāḇōʾ mōšeh vaydabbēr ʾet-kol-diḇrê haššîrāh-hazzōʾt — 'Moses came and spoke all the words of this song.' The narrative frame resumes: Moses and Joshua (yĕhôšuaʿ — here called hôšēaʿ, his original name before Moses renamed him) recited the song to the assembled Israel. The mention of Joshua's presence at the song's delivery foreshadows the imminent transfer of leadership — the Song's message of covenant consequences would be the theological framework within which Joshua would lead the conquest. The song was not merely poetry but a prophetic document programing Israel's covenant future.
45: vaykkal mōšeh lĕdabbēr ʾet-kol-haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh — 'When Moses finished speaking all these words to all Israel.' The completion formula (vaykkal — H3615, to finish, complete) signals the end of Moses's final literary-prophetic composition. The phrase ʾel kol-yisrāʾel (to all Israel) is inclusive — the entire community was the audience and recipient. The Song's message was not for an elite but for the common people who would live through covenant blessings and curses. The pedagogical strategy (teaching the song so it would be memorized, Deuteronomy 31:19) ensured that the theological framework would persist even when Israel forgot the words of the Torah itself.
46: vayyōʾmer ʾălêhem śîmû lĕḇaḇĕkhem lĕkhol-haddĕḇārîm — 'He said to them: Set your heart on all the words.' The final instruction: śîm lēḇāḇ (to set the heart on — H3824 + H7760) means to internalize, not merely memorize. The purpose: tĕtsawwûm ʾet-bĕnêkhem lišmōr laʿăśôt ʾet-kol-diḇrê hattôrāh hazzōʾt (to command your children to keep and do all the words of this Torah). The transmission chain — parent to child — was the mechanism of covenant continuity. The reason: kî lōʾ-dāḇār rēq hûʾ mikkkem (for this is not an empty word for you). Moses denied that Torah was abstract or irrelevant — it was ḥayyêkhem (your life) and would extend your days in the land.
47: kî lōʾ-dāḇār rēq hûʾ mikkkem kî-hûʾ ḥayyêkhem — 'For this is not an empty word for you; it is your very life.' Dāḇār rēq (empty/idle word — H7387 rêq, empty, vain, H1697 dāḇār) is the accusation that Torah is merely ceremonial or irrelevant. Moses's rebuttal: hûʾ ḥayyêkhem (it is your life). The word ḥayyîm (H2416 — life, both biological and covenant life) is the ultimate summation of Torah's purpose. Deuteronomy 30:19 gave Israel the choice of life or death; now Moses specifies that the word itself IS the life. This verse anticipates Jesus's claim that 'man shall not live by bread alone but by every word from the mouth of God' (Matthew 4:4, quoting Deuteronomy 8:3) and John's identification of the Word with life (John 1:4; Deuteronomy 6:63).
48: vaydabbēr YHWH ʾel-mōšeh beʿetsem hayyôm hazzeh lēʾmōr — 'YHWH spoke to Moses on that very day, saying.' The phrase beʿetsem hayyôm hazzeh (on this very day — H6675) indicates immediacy — the divine command came precisely when Moses had finished addressing Israel. The day of final address was also the day of Moses's death summons. YHWH directed Moses to hahar hāʾăḇārîm hazzeh har-nĕḇô (this mountain of the Abarim, Mount Nebo) — the specific peak from which Moses would see the land and then die.
49: ʿălēh ʾel-har hāʾăḇārîm hazzeh har-nĕḇô — 'Go up to this mountain of the Abarim, Mount Nebo.' Har hāʾăḇārîm (Mountain of the Abarim — H5682, the heights across the Jordan from Jericho) is the trans-Jordanian plateau. Har-nĕḇô (Mount Nebo — H5015) was the specific peak, identified today with Jebel en-Neba (~810m elevation), from which the promised land was visible in panoramic view. Urêh ʾet-ʾerets kĕnaʿan (see the land of Canaan) — Moses would see what he could not enter. The reason recalled: the Waters of Meribah-Kadesh incident (Numbers 20:1-13), where Moses struck the rock instead of speaking to it, failing to demonstrate YHWH's holiness to Israel.
50: ûmût behāhār ʾăšer ʾattāh ʿōleh šāmmāh — 'Die on the mountain which you are going up.' The command to die (mût — H4191) is not a threat but a divine appointment. Wehēʾāsep ʾel-ʿammêkhā (be gathered to your people) is the honorable death formula for the covenant faithful (cf. Genesis 25:8 Abraham; Genesis 49:33 Jacob). Moses would die kaʾăšer-mēt ʾaharōn ʾāḥîkhā bĕhar-hōr (as Aaron died on Mount Hor — Numbers 20:28) — the precedent of the priest dying at his post on the mountain, handing off the office to his successor. Both brothers died in elevated places outside the promised land, their deaths marking the end of the wilderness generation's leadership.
51: ʿal ʾăšer mĕʿaltem bî bĕtôkh bĕnê yisrāʾel — 'Because you broke faith with me among the sons of Israel.' The word māʿal (H4603 — breach of faith, covenant treachery, the same word for priests embezzling sacred things) applied to Moses is severe. Moses's sin at Meribah was not simply anger but māʿal — a covenant violation that was communicable, leading Israel to fail to trust YHWH's holiness. The connection between Moses's failure and Israel's subsequent failures suggests that leadership sin had communal consequences. Moses died not for his own sake alone but as the bearer of Israel's corporate failure at Meribah.
52: kî minneged tirʾeh ʾet-hāʾārets weʾl-šāmmāh lōʾ tāḇôʾ — 'For you shall see the land only from a distance; you shall not enter it.' The vision without entry — minneged tirʾeh (you will see from opposite/afar — H5048 minneghed) but lōʾ tāḇôʾ šāmmāh (you will not enter there) — was the final gracious gift within the judgment: Moses would see the fulfillment of the promise even as the punishment stood. The divine refusal was not cruelty but consistency: the God who judged Israel for failing to trust His holiness could not exempt His own servant from the same covenant standard. The land ʾăšer-ʾănî nōtēn liḇnê yisrāʾel (which I am giving to the sons of Israel) — the promise was kept, just not through Moses.
